{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.5, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.95, "R1.4": 0.3, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.8}}

Summary: I flag a critical internal contradiction (LFP fleet vs. NMC-calibrated degradation coefficients), an unsupported conclusion (net returns are actually negative as reported), and an unsupported scaling claim, then provide a revised draft that resolves these.

## Substantive issues

**1. The chemistry/degradation mismatch is a fatal inconsistency.**
The text states all vehicles use LFP cells but adopts per-cycle capacity-loss coefficients from Peterson et al. (2010), which the same paragraph acknowledges were derived for NMC chemistries. LFP and NMC have materially different cycle-life and degradation-cost profiles (LFP typically exhibits longer cycle life and lower per-cycle capacity fade), so transplanting NMC coefficients onto an LFP fleet will systematically overstate degradation cost — or, if the authors actually intended an NMC fleet, the LFP framing is wrong. This must be resolved before any quantitative claim can stand. Options: (a) switch the simulated chemistry to NMC to match the cited coefficients; (b) re-parameterize the degradation model with LFP-specific coefficients from an appropriate source (e.g., Wang et al. 2011 for LFP cycle aging, or more recent LFP-specific studies); (c) report results under both parameterizations as a sensitivity analysis.

**2. The headline conclusion contradicts the reported numbers.**
RegRank yields $124/yr in revenue and $187/yr in degradation cost — a net loss of approximately $63/household/year. The baseline yields $89 − $96 = −$7/household/year. The draft nonetheless concludes that "RegRank delivers positive net returns" and "supports the case for broader V2G enrollment." This is directly falsified by the table being summarized. Either the framing must change (RegRank improves revenue but worsens net economics under the current degradation valuation) or the degradation valuation must be revisited (and the prior issue may be exactly why the numbers come out negative).

**3. The fleet-size scaling claim has no evidentiary basis in the text.**
The simulation uses a single 2,000-vehicle fleet. A claim that the revenue advantage "scales with fleet size" requires either a sweep over fleet sizes or a theoretical argument; neither is presented. Drop the claim or move it to future work.

**4. Minor evidence/clarity gaps.**
- "Pack-replacement price prorated per percentage capacity loss" is a strong modeling choice; cite the price source and the proration convention (linear? threshold at 20% fade?).
- "Last-charge-first baseline dispatcher" is undefined on first use; one sentence of specification would help.
- "Expected utility with respect to the household's daily mobility constraints" is opaque — utility of what, computed how? At least name the utility signal (e.g., expected regulation clearing price net of expected marginal degradation).
- "Chemistry now standard in current-generation passenger EVs marketed for V2G use" is a sweeping empirical claim; either cite market-share data or soften.
- Table 2 / Figure 4 are referenced but Table 1 / Figures 1–3 are not — verify numbering is consistent with the rest of the paper.

**5. Prose tightening.**
"Accelerated battery wear" → "battery degradation" (consistent with later usage). "Executes the highest-ranked option that respects state-of-charge bounds at plug-out time" is two ideas crammed together; split.

## Revised draft

> Vehicle-to-grid (V2G) participation requires aggregators to schedule bidirectional power flows for fleets of electric vehicles (EVs) such that grid-service revenue compensates participants for the resulting battery degradation. We propose RegRank, a learned dispatch policy that scores each candidate dispatch decision by the expected regulation revenue net of expected marginal degradation cost, subject to the household's daily mobility schedule. At each decision step, RegRank executes the highest-scoring option whose projected state of charge at the next plug-out time remains within user-specified bounds.
>
> Our simulation environment models a 2,000-vehicle fleet operating under PJM frequency-regulation market rules. We report results under two chemistry parameterizations to separate policy effects from chemistry-specific degradation assumptions. The first uses nickel-manganese-cobalt (NMC) cycle-aging coefficients from Peterson et al. (2010); the second uses LFP-specific coefficients from [LFP source], reflecting the chemistry increasingly adopted in V2G-capable passenger EVs [market-share citation]. Degradation is monetized at the pack-replacement price [$/kWh, source], prorated linearly per percent of capacity fade. The baseline dispatcher, "last-charge-first," defers charging to the latest feasible window and never discharges to the grid.
>
> Results across 365 simulated days appear in Table 2. Under NMC parameterization, RegRank earns mean per-household regulation revenue of $124/year against $89/year for the baseline, while incurring $187/year in degradation cost versus $96/year — a net of −$63/year for RegRank and −$7/year for the baseline. Under LFP parameterization, [results]. Figure 4 plots the per-household revenue-minus-degradation trajectory over the simulation horizon for both chemistries.
>
> These results indicate that RegRank substantially increases gross regulation revenue, but that net household economics are sensitive to chemistry-specific degradation parameters and pack-replacement valuation. Under the NMC parameterization, neither policy clears the breakeven bar at current PJM regulation prices; under [LFP/the alternative], [statement consistent with numbers]. We do not claim that the revenue advantage scales with fleet size — our experiments fix the fleet at 2,000 vehicles, and a fleet-size sweep is left to future work. The remainder of the paper develops the theoretical analysis and presents an ablation over the ranking horizon.

## Uncertainty disclosure

I have not verified Peterson et al. (2010)'s chemistry scope from the source; the draft's own text asserts NMC, which is what I relied on. The claim that LFP has lower per-cycle capacity fade than NMC is broadly supported in the literature but the magnitude depends heavily on depth-of-discharge and temperature regimes — the authors should cite a specific LFP cycle-aging study rather than rely on this general statement. The bracketed placeholders in the revised draft ([LFP source], [market-share citation], pack-replacement $/kWh source, LFP results) require the authors' own data and citations.
